description: Nepali homographs for TTS evaluation (NepTTS-Bench style) — words spelled identically in Devanagari but with different pronunciations or meanings depending on context. Modeled after the Blizzard Challenge 2023 homograph evaluation. Tests whether a TTS system can use sentential context to select the correct pronunciation.

notes.usage: Each entry provides a homograph with two or more readings, each disambiguated by an example sentence. Use these to evaluate whether a TTS system produces the correct pronunciation based on context.
notes.evaluation: A good TTS system should produce distinct pronunciations for each reading when given the disambiguating sentence. Evaluate by comparing synthesized audio against the target pronunciation field.
notes.ambiguity_types: Covers schwa deletion, tatsama vs tadbhava pronunciation, part-of-speech dependent pronunciation, stress/emphasis differences, and Nepali-Hindi cognate divergences
notes.schwa_deletion_note: Nepali inherits the Devanagari inherent vowel 'a' (schwa), but schwa deletion rules are context-dependent and not always predictable from spelling alone. This is the single largest source of homographic ambiguity in Nepali.

readings.example_english: The government imposed a new tax.

homographs.description: With schwa deleted, /kar/ means 'tax'; with schwa retained, /kara/ is the informal imperative of गर्नु (to do). Sentence context (noun vs. verb position) disambiguates.

readings.example_english: The fruit on the tree has ripened.
The result of hard work is sweet.

homographs.description: Both are nouns, but /phal/ (with deleted schwa) typically means 'fruit' while /phala/ (with retained schwa) means 'result/consequence' in formal registers. The distinction is subtle and context-dependent.

readings.example_english: He has a lot of strength.
Turn on the light, it's dark.

homographs.description: Schwa deletion distinguishes the noun /bal/ (strength) from the verb imperative /bala/ (burn/light up). Syntactic position disambiguates.

readings.example_english: We suffered a defeat in that game.

homographs.description: As a determiner /har/ (every), schwa is deleted; as a noun /hara/ (defeat), schwa is retained. Part of speech and sentence position disambiguate.

The Sanskrit-origin word विद्या has two readings: colloquial /bidyā/ (with /b/ for /v/) used in everyday speech, and formal /vidyā/ (with labiodental /v/) used in literary, academic, or religious contexts. Register determines pronunciation.

readings.example_english: There is a difference between poison and nectar.

homographs.description: Sanskrit विष is pronounced /biṣ/ in colloquial Nepali (labial /b/ for labiodental /v/) but /viṣ/ in formal, literary, or religious contexts. The surrounding register markers determine which reading is appropriate.

readings.example_english: There is a tiger in the forest.

readings.example_sentence: वन तथा वातावरण मन्त्रालयले निर्णय गर्यो।
readings.example_english: The Ministry of Forests and Environment made a decision.

homographs.description: वन (forest) is /ban/ in everyday speech but /van/ in official/formal contexts (e.g., वन मन्त्रालय). Government and institutional contexts trigger the Sanskritic reading.

readings.example_english: We need to go to the other side of the river.
Bow down (fall in prostration) to the teacher.

homographs.description: Schwa-deleted /par/ is a noun/adverb meaning 'far side', while schwa-retained /para/ is an imperative verb. Syntactic role disambiguates.

Same spelling and base pronunciation, but greeting नमस्ते typically has a rising/warm intonation contour, while farewell नमस्ते has a falling/conclusive contour. Sentence-final vs. sentence-initial position also signals the difference.

readings.example_english: Let's eat lentil soup and rice today.
That political party won the election.

homographs.description: Both readings are nouns, but the food meaning दाल is often written with long vowel while the political meaning दल has short vowel. When written as दल, context (food vs. politics) disambiguates the pronunciation.

readings.example_english: Please give one mana of rice.

homographs.description: Schwa-deleted /man/ means 'mind/heart'; schwa-retained /mana/ is a traditional Nepali unit of weight/volume measurement. Context of emotion vs. commerce disambiguates.
Schwa-deleted /kal/ typically means 'tap/faucet' in domestic contexts; schwa-retained /kala/ means 'machine/engine' in industrial contexts. Semantic domain disambiguates.
As a noun /jal/ (water, formal), schwa is deleted; as a verb imperative /jala/ (burn), schwa is retained. Part of speech and syntactic position disambiguate.

readings.example_english: Corpses are cremated at the ghat.

homographs.description: The determiner 'all' /sab/ has deleted schwa, while the noun 'corpse' /saba/ retains it (often written शव in formal contexts). Context of quantification vs. death/cremation disambiguates.

readings.example_english: Faith is the foundation of human life.

homographs.description: Colloquial pronunciation uses /b/ for /v/: /bishwās/. Formal, religious, or literary contexts use the Sanskritic /vishwās/. Register and formality level determine the reading.

readings.example_english: He left all his household behind.

homographs.description: Standalone /ghar/ (schwa deleted) means 'house'; in certain compound forms like घरबार, the schwa may be partially retained /ghara-bār/. Context of standalone vs. compound usage affects pronunciation.

Same pronunciation for both meanings, but prosodic emphasis differs: 'water' (drink context) has neutral prosody, while 'rain' (weather context) may carry heavier stress. TTS must pick appropriate prosody from context.

readings.example_english: Development is happening in the village.
The Ministry of Development made a plan.

homographs.description: Like other व-initial Sanskrit words, colloquial Nepali uses /b/ (/bikās/) while formal/institutional contexts use /v/ (/vikās/). The presence of official titles or formal register triggers the Sanskritic reading.
As a postposition meaning 'throughout', /bhar/ has deleted schwa and attaches to nouns (दिनभर). As a verb imperative /bhara/ (fill), schwa is retained. Grammatical function disambiguates.

readings.example_english: The male lion is the king of the forest.

readings.example_sentence: नर र नारायणको कथा पुराणमा छ।
readings.example_english: The story of Nara and Narayana is in the Puranas.

homographs.description: As an adjective 'male' /nar/ (schwa deleted); as a mythological/literary proper noun 'Nara' /nara/ (schwa retained). Context of everyday gender vs. religious narrative disambiguates.
Medical/everyday context gives /tap/ (fever/heat); religious/literary context gives /tapa/ (penance/austerity) with schwa retained. Semantic domain disambiguates.
In wedding/colloquial context, /bar/ with labial /b/; in religious/mythological formal context, /var/ with labiodental /v/. The wedding vs. divine context triggers different readings.
Noun /sun/ (gold) has deleted schwa; verb imperative /suna/ (listen) retains schwa. Extremely common ambiguity in everyday Nepali; syntactic position (object vs. predicate) disambiguates.
Noun /din/ (day) vs. verbal stem /dina/ (to give). Very high-frequency ambiguity. Preceding and following words determine part of speech.
As a prefix/compound element /jan/ (people, as in जनता, जनसेवा), schwa is deleted. As a classifier /jana/ (counting people), schwa is retained. Compound vs. standalone usage disambiguates.

readings.example_english: Where is your place of residence?

homographs.description: Everyday colloquial usage gives /bās/ (with /b/); formal or compound usage (वासस्थान, निवास) gives /vās/ (with /v/). Register and compound context determine the reading.

readings.example_english: Let's go now, it's getting late.

homographs.description: Noun /chal/ (trick/stratagem) vs. verb imperative /chala/ (move/go). Very common ambiguity resolved by syntactic position and surrounding verbs.

readings.example_english: A Satyanarayan religious discourse was held at the temple.

homographs.description: Same spelling and base pronunciation, but secular 'story' has neutral prosody while religious 'katha' (recitation) carries a more reverential, measured prosodic pattern. Semantic context (entertainment vs. temple/ritual) disambiguates.

readings.example_english: Put a stamp on the paper.

homographs.description: Noun /chhāp/ (stamp/seal) vs. verb imperative /chhāpa/ (print). Classic schwa-deletion noun-verb pair in Nepali.

readings.example_english: Last year we went to Pokhara.
After the departed soul left, the funeral procession was carried out.

homographs.description: As a common adjective meaning 'last/previous' (गत वर्ष, गत हप्ता), /gat/ has deleted schwa. In literary/Sanskrit compounds like गतप्राण (departed soul), schwa may be retained as /gata/. Register and compound context determine the reading.
